flashcards.front: Define the Three-Schema Architecture in DBMS
flashcards.back: A three-level database architecture that separates user views (External Schema) from the logical database structure (Conceptual Schema) and physical storage (Internal Schema), enabling data independence and multiple user perspectives.

The user-level schema that describes how specific users or user groups view the database. It provides data security by exposing only relevant data, supports personalized views, and multiple external schemas can coexist for different applications.

The community view representing the entire database structure for all users. It defines all entities, attributes, relationships, and integrity constraints without any storage details — serving as the bridge between external and internal schemas.

flashcards.front: What does the Internal Schema specify?
flashcards.back: The physical-level description defining how data is actually stored: storage structures (files, indexes), access methods, data compression, encryption, and physical ordering — hidden from users and applications.

The ability to modify the schema at one level without affecting the schema at the next higher level. It ensures that changes in database structure don't require rewriting application programs, reducing maintenance costs.

Changes to the internal schema (physical storage, indexing, file organization) do not affect the conceptual schema. Example: switching from sequential to B-tree indexing — applications and logical structure remain unchanged.

Changes to the conceptual schema (adding new attributes, entities, or relationships) do not affect external schemas or application programs. Example: adding a 'phone_number' attribute to a 'Student' entity — existing user views remain valid.

flashcards.front: What is Schema Mapping in Three-Schema Architecture?
flashcards.back: Schema mapping defines correspondences between levels: External-to-Conceptual and Conceptual-to-Internal. These mappings enable data transformation and independence — when schemas change, mappings are updated rather than applications.
